en: The thing about Americans that I've thought about the language is that they speak... They say they speak English,
ko: 미국인들에 대해 제가 언어적으로 생각해본 건, 그들이 말하길... 영어를 한다고 하잖아요,

en: but they've had to change it to make them understand it more.
ko: 근데 자기들이 더 잘 이해하려고 바꿔버렸어요.

en: Go with me on this, because I've thought it through. They've changed some of the words.
ko: 한번 따라와 보세요, 저도 깊이 생각해봤거든요. 단어 몇 개를 바꿨어요.

en: So, they've taken the English language, but they've looked at some of it and they've thought,
ko: 그러니까, 영어를 가져다가 일부를 보고 이렇게 생각한 거예요,

en: no, no, I think we need a little bit more explanation here, OK?
ko: 아니, 아니, 여기 설명이 좀 더 필요한 것 같아, 알겠지?

en: That's my American accent. So, things like pavement.
ko: 이게 제 미국식 억양이에요. 그러니까, 'pavement' 같은 거요.

en: So, they can't work with pavement, so they've changed it to sidewalk.
ko: pavement으로는 안 되니까, sidewalk으로 바꿨어요.

en: They needed more information. They needed to know where they were going to be walking
ko: 정보가 더 필요했던 거예요. 어디서 걸어야 하는지 알아야 했으니까

en: so that they wouldn't get run over. So, I didn't think of that.
ko: 차에 안 치이려고요. 그건 생각 못 했네.

en: Pavement, sidewalk. There must have been a period of time where they ran with the word pavement,
ko: Pavement, sidewalk. pavement이라는 단어를 쓰던 시기가 있었을 텐데,

en: but they kept getting hit by cars. So, they changed it to sidewalk.
ko: 계속 차에 치였을 거예요. 그래서 sidewalk으로 바꿨죠.

en: Now, bear with me here, Jon, because I've thought this through.
ko: 자, 좀만 참아주세요 존, 이거 깊이 생각해봤거든요.

en: Bin. The word for bin. In American, waste paper basket.
ko: Bin. bin이라는 단어. 미국에선 waste paper basket이에요.

en: They needed to know not only what to put in it,
ko: 뭘 넣어야 하는지뿐만 아니라,

en: they needed to know that not only it was paper, but waste paper, and then it goes into a basket.
ko: 종이만이 아니라 버릴 종이라는 것도 알아야 했고, 바구니에 넣는다는 것도요.

en: It's not just any paper. They kept throwing away fresh paper for a period
ko: 아무 종이나 아니에요. 한동안 새 종이를 계속 버렸대요

en: before they introduced the word waste paper basket.
ko: waste paper basket이라는 말을 도입하기 전에요.

en: You see, it's like they need instructions. Glasses for your eyes.
ko: 보세요, 설명서가 필요한 거예요. 눈에 쓰는 glasses.

en: They called them eyeglasses. They needed to know where to put them on their face,
ko: 미국에선 eyeglasses라고 불렀어요. 얼굴 어디에 써야 하는지 알아야 했으니까,

en: because they used to have glasses, and they would put them on their thighs, and they would put them on their feet,
ko: 왜냐면 glasses가 있으면 허벅지에도 올리고, 발에도 올리고,

en: and they would say, I can't see any better with the glasses.
ko: 이러면서 말하겠죠, 이 안경 써도 더 안 보이는데.

en: And then somebody went, no, they're eyeglasses. Well, why didn't you call them eyeglasses?
ko: 그러면 누군가가, 아니 그건 eyeglasses야. 그럼 왜 처음부터 eyeglasses라고 안 했어?

en: There's a game called Squash. Have you heard of it? Yes.
ko: 스쿼시라는 운동 있잖아요. 들어보셨죠? 네.

en: Not in America. Racketball. They needed... It's a true story.
ko: 미국에선 아니에요. 라켓볼이에요. 그들은... 실화예요.

en: They needed to know what they were going to be playing with, a racketball, and even then they get confused,
ko: 뭘로 치는지 알아야 했어요, 라켓볼, 근데 그래도 헷갈려해요,

en: because there's no court, they don't know where to go. They just wander through the street going,
ko: 코트가 없으니까, 어디로 가야 할지 모르니까. 그냥 거리를 돌아다니면서,

en: I want to play racketball, I want to play racketball. So the company had to change that.
ko: 라켓볼 하고 싶어, 라켓볼 하고 싶어. 그래서 회사에서 바꿔야 했어요.

en: But my favourite one, without a shadow of a doubt, is horse riding.
ko: 근데 제가 제일 좋아하는 건, 의심의 여지 없이, 승마예요.

en: Do you know what horse riding is in America? Is it not called horse riding? No. Horseback riding.
ko: 미국에서 승마를 뭐라고 하는지 아세요? horse riding 아니에요? 아니요. Horseback riding이에요.

en: They had to specify the area of the horse.
ko: 말의 어느 부위인지 특정해야 했어요.

en: Well, yes. There were terrible problems, because there was a period of time
ko: 네, 맞아요. 끔찍한 문제가 있었거든요, 왜냐면 한동안

en: when they didn't call it horseback riding. Well, where did they go?
ko: horseback riding이라고 안 부르던 때가 있었으니까요. 그럼 어디에 올라탔냐면?

en: They used to hold on to the tail...
ko: 꼬리를 잡고...

en: Are you sure this is how they ride the horses in Europe?
ko: 유럽에서 진짜 그렇게 말 타는 거 맞아요?

en: That's a whole different thing you're doing there, I think, with the horse, aren't you?
ko: 그건 완전히 다른 거 하는 거 같은데요, 말이랑, 그렇죠?

en: Anybody who's watching this show who may have been forced
ko: 이 쇼를 보고 있는 분 중에 어쩌다

en: into flicking the channels, that's not a moment you want to call away.
ko: 채널을 돌리게 된 분이 있다면, 지금 이 순간은 소리 지르면 안 됩니다.

en: No, no, no. This was a story about Americans and descriptive words.
ko: 아니, 아니, 아니요. 이건 미국인들과 설명적인 단어에 대한 이야기였어요.

en: I've never been in the Mile High Club.
ko: 저는 마일 하이 클럽에 가본 적이 없어요.

en: So, wait. Harry, what are you waiting for? Come on, we know the answer.
ko: 그래서, 잠깐. 해리, 뭘 기다리는 거야? 어서, 우리 답 알잖아.

en: But who was the closest to him in the band?
ko: 근데 밴드에서 누가 그와 가장 가까웠어요?

en: I think when we were in the band, yeah, maybe it was me.
ko: 밴드에 있을 때는, 응, 아마 나였을 거야.

en: And, you know, I think we all kind of noticed that he wasn't thoroughly enjoying it, and we were.
ko: 있잖아, 우리 모두 그가 완전히 즐기지 못하고 있다는 걸 느꼈던 것 같아, 우리는 즐기고 있었는데.

en: And, you know, it can be an intense job at times, and I think it got a little bit too much for him,
ko: 있잖아, 때때로 꽤 힘든 일이 될 수 있는데, 그에게는 좀 과했던 것 같아,

en: and he wanted to be in a different place, and that's fair enough.
ko: 그리고 다른 곳에 있고 싶었던 거지, 그건 충분히 이해할 수 있어.
